next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

By the time Anika double-knotted her shoes, the old HVAC above Court Two had started its metal cough, three hard rattles and one long whine, like somebody dragging a chair across tin. The sound bounced inside the public rec center and settled in her teeth. She checked Maya's watch out of habit, then corrected herself because it was not a habit yet, not really. She had started wearing it only this week, and the strap still sat wrong on her wrist bone unless she pushed it up with her thumb.

6:43 p.m.

Ravi stood by the cinder-block wall with a clipboard he did not need. He had printed a match sheet from work and clipped it to the board even though the ladder organizer had his own forms. He liked paper that could be held. He liked columns. He liked an object that did not answer back. A smear of bus-depot grease stained the side of his left thumb, and he kept rubbing it with his right index finger as if he could erase it before anyone noticed.

"You warm up through the middle," he said. "Don't chase early winners. Make her hit one more ball every rally. She wants you rushing. Don't give it."

Anika nodded once.

"Say it."

"Middle first. One more ball."

"And your serve routine."

"Four bounces, breath out, eyes up."

He looked at the watch on her wrist, then at her face, and he did not mention Maya. He never mentioned Maya before matches. It was one of his rules that was not written anywhere.

Across the glass, the ladder board listed names in dry-erase marker. ANIKA IYER sat under RUNG 7. Tonight she played for RUNG 6. Above her name was LEAH SETHI, fourteen, Fo...
open_hooks_to_carry: Donation sorting scene with conflicting tempos: Ravi fast, Anika stalled.
Maya's scent and handwriting trigger concrete memory flashes.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...athing diesel. She wedges into the aisle, one hand on the wet pole, and texts a photo through the fogged window as proof.

Dad: `Good.`
Dad: `Sip water now.`

She obeys automatically, unscrewing the bottle with numb fingers. The water tastes like plastic and metal and relief. At the next light, two girls from school cross in front of the bus sharing earbuds, laughing so hard one of them has to grab the other's sleeve to stay upright. Anika watches them until the doors hiss shut again.

She opens her notes app and writes what she tells herself is logistics:

`social studies asleep 8:19`
`Ms D emailed`
`lunch finished (not true)`
`Z invited park 3:15 said no`
`gate 3:24`

The list sits there like evidence. She reads the line with not true and does not delete it.

By the time the rec center comes into view, the sky is already dimming behind the chain-link fence. The building's windows throw back a dull yellow, and she can almost hear the rubber-ball echo before the doors open. She slides the phone into her bag, squares her shoulders, and steps off the bus.

The lie is not just in what she told Zoe. It is in what she told Ms. DeMarco, and Ravi, and herself. "I'm fine" becomes fine and then becomes nothing at all, a smooth stone she keeps in her mouth so no other words can get out.

Inside the lobby, she sees Ravi by the vending machine, still in depot uniform, reflective stripes at his cuffs, eyes on the clock. He lifts his hand once in a hurry signal and starts toward the courts before she reaches him. She follows.

The social part of her day has already ended. It is 3:58 p.m.
state_deltas_required: Help clear Maya's belongings while keeping at least one private link to her.
Anika secretly keeps Maya's watch and a half-used notebook, creating a hidden ledger that tracks food, reps, and grief spikes.
